lesson_title: Overloading the arithmetic operators using friend functions

cards.text: When overloading a binary operator as a friend function, you need {{c1::two}} parameters: one for the left operand and one for the right operand.
cards.source: 21.2 - Overloading the arithmetic operators using friend functions

cards.text:
Friend function to overload operator+:
{{c1::friend}} Cents {{c2::operator+}}(const Cents& c1, const Cents& c2);
cards.source: 21.2 - Overloading the arithmetic operators using friend functions

cards.front: Why use friend functions for operator overloading instead of member functions?
cards.back:
Friend functions are preferred when:
  • The left operand is not of your class type (e.g., int + Cents)
  • Both operands should be treated symmetrically
  • You need to access private members but don't want a member function
  • The operator needs to work with implicit conversions on the left operand
cards.source: 21.2 - Overloading the arithmetic operators using friend functions

cards.front: What should arithmetic operators (+, -, *, /) typically return?
cards.back: Arithmetic operators should typically return a new object by value, not a reference. This is because the result is a temporary object, and returning a reference to a local variable would be undefined behavior.
cards.source: 21.2 - Overloading the arithmetic operators using friend functions

cards.question: What's the typical pattern for implementing operator+ as a friend?
cards.answer: Declare friend function inside class, define it outside. Takes two const references, returns new object by value containing the sum.
cards.explanation: The friend declaration gives the function access to private members. Parameters are const ref for efficiency. Returns by value since result is a new object.
cards.source: 21.2 - Overloading the arithmetic operators using friend functions

cards.text: Arithmetic operator parameters should be passed by {{c1::const reference}} to avoid unnecessary copies and prevent modification of operands.
cards.source: 21.2 - Overloading the arithmetic operators using friend functions

cards.front: Should you implement operator+= if you've implemented operator+?
cards.back: Yes. Compound assignment operators (+=, -=, *=, /=) are separate from arithmetic operators and should be implemented independently. They modify the left operand in-place and typically return a reference to *this.
cards.source: 21.2 - Overloading the arithmetic operators using friend functions

cards.text: Binary operators (like +, -, *, /) take {{c1::two}} parameters when implemented as friend functions, while unary operators (like -, !) take {{c2::one}} parameter.
cards.source: 21.2 - Overloading the arithmetic operators using friend functions

cards.question: Why does this work with friend functions but not member functions?
cards.answer: Friend functions allow implicit conversion on both operands. With member functions, only the right operand can be implicitly converted (c1 + 5 works, but 5 + c1 doesn't).
cards.explanation: Friend operator+(const Cents&, const Cents&) allows the compiler to convert int to Cents for either parameter, providing symmetry.
cards.source: 21.2 - Overloading the arithmetic operators using friend functions

cards.concept: Best practices for overloading arithmetic operators
cards.explanation:
Follow these guidelines when overloading arithmetic operators:
  • Use friend functions for symmetry and implicit conversions
  • Parameters: pass by const reference
  • Return: by value (new object)
  • Don't modify operands
  • Implement related operators together (+, +=, -, -=, etc.)
  • Make behavior consistent with built-in types
examples: friend Cents operator+(const Cents& c1, const Cents& c2);  // Good
Cents operator+(Cents c1, Cents c2);  // Bad: copies
void operator+(const Cents& c1, const Cents& c2);  // Bad: returns void
cards.source: 21.2 - Overloading the arithmetic operators using friend functions

cards.front: What are the four basic arithmetic operators typically overloaded together?
cards.back:
• operator+ (addition)
  • operator- (subtraction)
  • operator* (multiplication)
  • operator/ (division)
Each should return a new object by value and not modify operands.
cards.source: 21.2 - Overloading the arithmetic operators using friend functions
